messages.content: لطفا این جمله را به انگلیسی بازیابی کنید. CV was used to examine unmodified CPE, GO/CPE, and MOF-235/GO/CPE in the electrochemical reaction of DAN (??? µM) in PBS (0.1 M) at pH 7.0. The recorded cyclic voltammograms are depicted in Figure 4. As can be seen, each CV curve showed a pair of redox peaks, indicating that DAN undergoes a reversible electrochemical process under these conditions. The unmodified CPE (voltammogram a) showed the high anodic (Epa) and cathodic (Epc) peak potentials and lower anodic (Ipa) and cathodic (Ipc) peak currentd, due to the sluggish electron transfer towards DAN. By using GO/CPE (voltammogram b) higher current responses of redox peaks were obtained. However, compared to unmodified CPE and GO/CPE, the MOF-235/GO nanocomposite modified CPE (voltammogram c) considerably enhanced the oxidation and reduction currents of DAN. These observations can be attributed to the suitable catalytic effect of MOF-235/GO nanocomposite for redox reaction of DAN.

CV ، GO/CPE و MOF-235/GO/CPE برای بررسی واکنش الکتروشیمیایی DAN (???µM) در PBS (0.1M) با pH 7.0 استفاده شد. ولتامتروگرام های دوره ای ثبت شده در شکل 4 نشان داده شده است. همانطور که مشاهده می شود ، هر منحنی CV جفتی از قله های الکتروشیمیایی را نشان می دهد ، که نشان می دهد که DAN در این شرایط یک فرایند الکتروشیمیایی قابل برگشت را طی می کند. CPE بدون تغییر (ولتاموگرام a) ، پتانسیلها و جریانهای قلهی اندودی (Epa) و کاتودیک (Epc) بالاتر و جریانهای قلهی اندودی (Ipa) و کاتودیک (Ipc) پایینتری را نشان داد ، به دلیل انتقال الکترون با سختی به سوی DAN. با استفاده از GO/CPE (ولتاموگرام b) پاسخهای جریانی بالاتر از قله های الکتروشیمیایی حاصل شد. ولی ، نسبت به CPE بدون تغییر و GO/CPE ، سیستم کامپوزیت MOF-235/GO مودیفی شده CPE (ولتاموگرام c) جریانهای اکسایش و کاهشی DAN را به طرز قابل توجهی افزایش داد. این مشاهدات می توانند به اثر کاتالیزور مناسب MOF-235/GO برای واکنش اکسیداسیون و کاهشی DAN نسبت داده شوند.

لطفا این جمله را به انگلیسی بازیابی  کنید. CV was used to examine unmodified CPE, GO/CPE, and MOF-235/GO/CPE in the electrochemical reaction of DAN (??? µM) in PBS (0.1 M) at pH 7.0. The recorded cyclic voltammograms are depicted in Figure 4.

CV was utilized to investigate the electrochemical reaction of DAN (??? µM) in PBS (0.1 M) at pH 7.0 by examining unmodified CPE, GO/CPE, and MOF-235/GO/CPE. The cyclic voltammograms obtained from the experiment are illustrated in Figure 4.
As can be seen, each CV curve showed a pair of redox peaks, indicating that DAN undergoes a reversible electrochemical process under these conditions.
As evident from the data, each cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve displayed a pair of redox peaks, suggesting that DAN experiences a reversible electrochemical process under the given circumstances.

The unmodified CPE (voltammogram a) showed the high anodic (Epa) and cathodic (Epc) peak potentials and lower anodic (Ipa) and cathodic (Ipc) peak currentd, due to the sluggish electron transfer towards DAN.

The unmodified CPE (voltammogram a) exhibited higher anodic (Epa) and cathodic (Epc) peak potentials, as well as lower anodic (Ipa) and cathodic (Ipc) peak currents. This can be attributed to the slow electron transfer towards DAN in this case.

By using GO/CPE (voltammogram b) higher current responses of redox peaks were obtained. However, compared to unmodified CPE and GO/CPE, the MOF-235/GO nanocomposite modified CPE (voltammogram c) considerably enhanced the oxidation and reduction currents of DAN.

By employing GO/CPE (voltammogram b), greater current responses of redox peaks were achieved. However, in comparison to unmodified CPE and GO/CPE, the MOF-235/GO nanocomposite modified CPE (voltammogram c) significantly amplified the oxidation and reduction currents of DAN.
These observations can be attributed to the suitable catalytic effect of MOF-235/GO nanocomposite for redox reaction of DAN.
These observations can be ascribed to the effective catalytic effect of the MOF-235/GO nanocomposite on the redox reaction of DAN.
The impact of scan rate (υ) on the redox reaction of ??? µM DAN in pH = 7.0 PBS (0.1 M) at MOF-235/GO/CPE was investigated (Figure 5).
The influence of scan rate (υ) on the redox reaction of ??? µM DAN in pH = 7.0 PBS (0.1 M) at MOF-235/GO/CPE was examined, and the results are depicted in Figure 5.
The Ipa and Ipc increased when scan rates rose from 5 to 100 mV s−1, according to analysis of DAN by CV (Figure 5).
The peak currents (Ipa and Ipc) of the redox reaction of DAN, as analyzed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) in Figure 5, exhibited an increase as the scan rates increased from 5 to 100 mV s−1.

Alternatively, the findings demonstrated that both peak currents (Ipa & Ipc) increased linearly with the square root of υ (υ1/2) for the drug DAN (Figure 5 (Inset)), confirming the explored range of potential scan rates and the diffusion-controlled process for redox reaction of DAN on the MOF-235/GO/CPE.

Alternatively, the results indicated that the peak currents (Ipa and Ipc) showed a linear increase with the square root of scan rate (υ1/2) for the DAN drug, as illustrated in Figure 5 (Inset). This confirms the range of potential scan rates investigated and suggests a diffusion-controlled process for the redox reaction of DAN on the MOF-235/GO/CPE.

Adjusting the potential of the working electrode at ؟؟؟ V at a surface of MOF-235/GO/CPE in PBS at a pH value of 7.0 allowed researchers to study the chronoamperometric behaviors of different concentrations of DAN (Figure 6).

By setting the potential of the working electrode at ??? V on the MOF-235/GO/CPE surface in PBS at a pH value of 7.0, researchers were able to investigate the chronoamperometric behaviors of various concentrations of DAN, as depicted in Figure 6.
The Cottrell equation describes the electrochemical reaction's current (at a mass transport restricted rate) for an electroactive substance (DAN in this case) with a diffusion coefficient of D:

The Cottrell equation describes the current of an electrochemical reaction (under mass transport-limited conditions) for an electroactive substance like DAN, with a diffusion coefficient of D:

I = (nFACD^(1/2))/(π^(1/2)t^(1/2))

where:
I: current (A)
n: number of electrons involved in the reaction
F: Faraday's constant (C/mol)
A: area of the electrode (cm²)
C: concentration of the electroactive substance (mol/cm³)
D: diffusion coefficient (cm²/s)
t: time (s)
D stands for diffusion coefficient (cm2 s-1) and Cb for applied bulk concentration (mol cm-3) in the Cottrell equation.

Apologies for the confusion. You are correct. In the Cottrell equation, D represents the diffusion coefficient (cm^2 s^-1) and Cb represents the applied bulk concentration (mol cm^-3). Thank you for clarifying.
I vs. t-1/2 experimental findings have been presented in Figure 6A, with the ideal matches for different DAN concentrations
Experimental results for I vs. t^(-1/2) have been shown in Figure 6A, along with the corresponding ideal matches for various concentrations of DAN.
The electrocatalytic current's linearity with respect to t-1/2 suggests that DAN must diffuse from the majority of the solution to the surface of the electrode in order to control the current.

The linear relationship between the electrocatalytic current and t^(-1/2) indicates that the diffusion of DAN from the bulk solution to the electrode surface governs the current. This suggests that the current is controlled by the diffusion process of DAN.

Following that, the slopes that corresponded to the lines in Figure 6A that have been drawn straight were plotted against the DAN concentration (Figure 6B). The resulting slope and the Cottrell equation were used to calculate the mean value of D, which has been discovered to be ?????????? cm2/s.

Subsequently, the slopes corresponding to the straight lines plotted in Figure 6A were plotted against the DAN concentration in Figure 6B. Using the obtained slope and the Cottrell equation, the mean value of D was calculated and found to be ?????????? cm^2/s.

Using DPV at MOF-235/GO/CPE, it has been determined whether the prepared sensor could be used for a quantitative analysis of DAN by looking at the correlation of the Ipa and the concentration of DAN (Figure 7).

By employing Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV) on the MOF-235/GO/CPE, it was investigated whether the developed sensor could be utilized for quantitative analysis of DAN. This was achieved by examining the correlation between the anodic peak current (Ipa) and the concentration of DAN, as portrayed in Figure 7.
The calibration curve (Figure 7 (Inset)) under optimized circumstances displayed a linear range from 0.005 to 400.0 μM with equations Ipa (µA) = 0.0152 CDAN (μM) + 0.9772 (R2 = 0.995).

The calibration curve, shown in Figure 7 (Inset) under optimized conditions, exhibited a linear range from 0.005 to 400.0 µM with the equation Ipa (µA) = 0.0152 CDAN (µM) + 0.9772 (R^2 = 0.995). This indicates a strong correlation between the anodic peak current (Ipa) and the concentration of DAN.

The increase in DAN concentration from 0.005 to 400.0 μM results in a linear increase in the peak current. Using the calibration plot, a detection limit of ??? μM (based on S/N = 3) was calculated for DAN.

As the concentration of DAN increased from 0.005 to 400.0 μM, there was a linear increase in the peak current. Utilizing the calibration plot, a detection limit of ??? μM (based on S/N = 3) was calculated for DAN.

The good activity of MOF-235/GO/CPE permits the DPV method's simultaneous detection of DAN and DTIC (Figure 8). Using MOF-235/GO/CPE, two distinct anodic peaks associated with the electrooxidation of DAN and DTIC were seen on DPV curves

The high activity of MOF-235/GO/CPE enables the simultaneous detection of both DAN and DTIC using the DPV method, as illustrated in Figure 8. The DPV curves exhibited two distinct anodic peaks corresponding to the electrooxidation of DAN and DTIC.
Furthermore, two DAN and DTIC peaks at ??? V and ??? V, respectively, were identified in the DPV responses.
Moreover, two peaks corresponding to DAN and DTIC were identified in the DPV responses at ??? V and ??? V, respectively.
The outcomes show that the two medicines can be distinguished well in combination solutions.
The results indicate that the two drugs, DAN and DTIC, can be effectively distinguished in combined solutions.
The Ipa of DAN and Ipa of DTIC increased linearly with concentration at the concentration region  (Figure 8A, 8B).
The anodic peak current (Ipa) of DAN and DTIC displayed a linear increase with concentration within the concentration range depicted in Figure 8A and 8B, respectively.
As shown, two distinct peaks with ∆E ~ ??? mV were found at a surface of MOF-235/GO/CPE for DAN and DTIC, which is sufficient for the simultaneous study of two anticancer drugs.

As observed, two clearly distinguishable peaks with a potential difference (∆E) of approximately ??? mV were observed on the surface of MOF-235/GO/CPE for both DAN and DTIC. This level of separation is adequate for simultaneous analysis of these two anticancer drugs.
Additionally, levels of sensitivity to DAN both when DTIC is present and absent have been discovered to be 0.0152 µA/µM (in the absence of DTIC) and 0.0153 µA/µM (in the presence of DTIC).
Furthermore, the sensitivity levels for DAN were determined to be 0.0152 µA/µM in the absence of DTIC and 0.0153 µA/µM in the presence of DTIC.
As a result, utilizing the modified electrode in matrix samples allows for the determination of these compounds at the same time.
Consequently, utilizing the modified electrode in matrix samples enables the simultaneous determination of these compounds.
